title: On the Implicit Biases of Architecture & Gradient Descent

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ON THE IMPLICIT BIASES OF ARCHITECTURE & GRADIENT DESCENT

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Do neural networks generalise because of bias in the functions returned by gradient descent, or bias already present in the network architecture?

## _¿Por qué no los dos?_

This paper finds that while typical networks that fit the training data already generalise fairly well, gradient descent can further improve generalisation by selecting networks with a large margin. This conclusion is based on a careful study of the behaviour of infinite width networks trained by Bayesian inference and finite width networks trained by gradient descent. To measure the implicit bias of architecture, new technical tools are developed to both _analytically bound_ and _consistently estimate_ the average test error of the neural network–Gaussian process (NNGP) posterior. This error is found to be already better than chance, corroborating the findings of Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) and underscoring the importance of architecture. Going beyond this result, this paper finds that test performance can be substantially improved by selecting a function with much larger margin than is typical under the NNGP posterior. This highlights a curious fact: _minimum a posteriori_ functions can generalise best, and gradient descent can select for those functions. In summary, new technical tools suggest a nuanced portrait of generalisation involving both the implicit biases of architecture and gradient descent.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Following an influential paper by Zhang et al. (2017), the basic question of _why neural networks generalise_ is generally regarded to be open. The authors demonstrate a surprising fact: deep learning generalises even when the neural network is expressive enough to represent functions that do not generalise. In turn, this implies that the theory of Vapnik & Chervonenkis (1971)—based on uniform convergence of train error to population error—does not explain generalisation in neural networks.

Several hypotheses have since been proposed to fill this theoretical vacuum. One prominent hypothesis states that, while most neural network solutions do not generalise well, there is an implicit bias in the kinds of functions returned by gradient descent (Soudry et al., 2018). In sharp contrast, a second hypothesis states that the solution space of a neural network is dominated by simple functions, while the complex kinds of functions that overfit are relatively rare (Valle-Pérez et al., 2019).

This latter hypothesis dovetails with a particular “PAC-Bayesian” theorem of McAllester (1998), which bounds the average population error of all classifiers consistent with a training sample. If this bound is small, then the complex functions that overfit must indeed be rare. In more technical terms, the PAC-Bayesian theorem can provide a meaningful certificate of generalisation even for machine learning models with an infinite Vapnik-Chervonenkis dimension, or an arbitrarily large number of parameters, by properly accounting for the measure of those very complex functions.

This paper takes a more nuanced position between these two hypotheses. While the _average_ population error of all neural networks that fit a training sample is found to be good (and _certifiably good_ by the PAC-Bayesian theorem), it is still possible for certain networks with special properties to perform substantially _better than average_ (and likewise substantially better than the PAC-Bayes bound). Moreover, gradient descent may be used to specifically target these special networks. This subtly counters a position put forward by Mingard et al. (2021), which suggests that gradient descent may be well-modelled as sampling randomly from a particular Bayesian posterior distribution.

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

To support these claims, a careful study of the behaviour of both infinite width and finite width neural networks is conducted. In particular, this paper makes the following technical contributions:

- Section 3 _Implicit Bias of Architecture_ . A purely analytical PAC-Bayes bound (Theorem 2) on the population error of the neural network–Gaussian process (NNGP) posterior for binary classification is derived. The bound furnishes an interpretable measure of model complexity that depends on both the architecture and the training data. This exact analytical bound improves upon an approximate computational approach due to Valle-Pérez et al. (2019).

- Section 4 _Testing the Bound_ . The new bound is found to be both non-vacuous and correlated with the test error of finite width multilayer perceptrons trained by gradient descent. This provides supporting evidence for the important role of architecture in generalisation, as put forward by Valle-Pérez et al. (2019). Still, a gap exists with the performance of gradient descent.

- Section 5 _Implicit Bias of Gradient Descent_ . Going further beyond the work of Valle-Pérez et al. (2019), a new theoretical tool (Theorem 3) is developed to enable consistent estimation of the average error of the NNGP posterior on a given holdout set. The average is found to be significantly worse than the holdout performance of Gaussian process draws with large margin. The experiment is repeated for finite width neural networks trained by gradient descent, and the same qualitative phenomenon persists. This finding demonstrates the ability of gradient descent to select large margin functions with vanishing posterior probability that nonetheless generalise significantly better than the posterior average.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Margin-based generalisation theory** A rich body of work explores the connection between margin and generalisation. For instance, Bartlett et al. (2017) propose a margin-based complexity measure for neural networks derived via Rademacher complexity analysis, and Neyshabur et al. (2018) derive a similar result via PAC-Bayes analysis. Neyshabur et al. (2019) test these bounds experimentally, finding them to be vacuous and to scale poorly with network width. Biggs & Guedj (2021) further develop this style of bound. Margin-based PAC-Bayes bounds go back at least to the work of Herbrich (2001), who derived such a bound for linear classification. The standard idea is that a solution with large margin implies the existence of nearby solutions with the same training error (but perhaps smaller margin), facilitating a more spread out PAC-Bayes posterior. This style of margin-based PAC-Bayes bound does not provide guidance on whether the original large margin classifier should generalise better or worse than the additional nearby classifiers included in the posterior.

**Non-vacuous bounds for neural networks** Aside from PAC-Bayes, many styles of generalisation bound for neural networks are vacuous (Dziugaite & Roy, 2017). Even many PAC-Bayes bounds are vacuous (Achille & Soatto, 2018; Foret et al., 2021) due to their choice of PAC-Bayes prior. Dziugaite & Roy (2017) construct a _non-vacuous_ weight space PAC-Bayes bound by iteratively optimising both the PAC-Bayes prior and posterior by gradient descent. Wu et al. (2020) extend this approach to involve Hessians. Meanwhile, Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) instantiate a non-vacuous PAC-Bayes bound in the function space of neural networks via the NNGP correspondence (Neal, 1994; Lee et al., 2018). The evaluation of this bound involves an iterative and statistically inconsistent expectation-propagation approximation. Unlike this paper’s purely analytical bound in Theorem 2, these non-vacuous bounds are all evaluated by iterative computational procedures.

**Implicit bias of gradient descent** Much research has gone into the role that gradient descent plays in neural network generalisation. For instance, Zhang et al. (2017) suggest that gradient descent may converge to solutions with special generalisation properties, while Wilson et al. (2017) suggest that gradient descent may have a more favourable implicit bias than the Adam optimiser. Azizan et al. (2021) make a related study in the context of mirror descent. Keskar et al. (2017) point to an implicit bias present in mini-batch stochastic gradient descent, but Geiping et al. (2021) argue against the importance of stochasticity. Meanwhile, Soudry et al. (2018) observe how gradient descent combined with certain loss functions converges to max-margin solutions; Wei et al. (2018) make a similar observation. On a different tack, Mingard et al. (2021), Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) and Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) argue that the implicit bias of neural architecture is more important than that of gradient descent for understanding generalisation, and so long as gradient descent does not select solutions pathologically, generalisation should occur.

## 3 IMPLICIT BIAS OF ARCHITECTURE

This section derives an analytical bound on the population error of an infinitely wide neural network averaged over all weight configurations that attain 0% train error—in other words, the average population error of the NNGP posterior. Since this quantity assesses the performance of all solutions rather than just those returned by gradient descent, it is intended to measure the implicit bias of architecture. In Section 4, the bound is found to be non-vacuous for multilayer perceptrons. The source of a substantial gap with the results of gradient descent training is investigated in Section 5, and ultimately attributed to an additional implicit bias of gradient descent: namely _margin_ .

## 3.1 PRELIMINARY NOTATION

A classifier _f_ shall depend on a weight vector _w ∈_ Ω, where Ω denotes the _weight space_ . In particular, for an input _x_ the prediction is given by sign _f_ ( _x_ ; _w_ ).

One is interested in the relationship between the _train error εS_ of a classifier on a dataset _S_ to the _population error εD_ on a data distribution _D_ . For a weight vector _w ∈_ Ω, these are defined as:
The _solution space VS_ := _{w ∈_ Ω _| εS_ ( _w_ ) = 0 _}_ denotes those classifiers that attain zero train error.

## 3.2 GEOMETRY OF SOLUTIONS IN FUNCTION SPACE

The function space _FS_ is defined to be the set of outputs that the classifier _f_ can realise on dataset _S_ :
In weight space the geometry of solutions can be arbitrarily complicated, but in function space the solutions occupy the single orthant picked out by the binary training labels.

To measure the volume of the solution space, one can define a measure P on weight space. It is convenient to enforce P[Ω] = 1 so that P is a probability measure. In the absence of a better alternative, P is usually set to a multivariate Gaussian or uniform distribution. The measure P on weight space induces a measure P on function space via the relation:
The volume of solutions is denoted P[ _VS_ ], and can be computed either in weight or function space. In function space, P[ _VS_ ] is an orthant probability. The situation is visualised in Figure 1.

## 3.3 PAC-BAYES THEORY

PAC-Bayes relates the volume of solutions to their average population error. The following result was derived by Valle-Pérez et al. (2019) as a corollary of a theorem due to Langford & Seeger (2001). The result is similar in form to a theorem of McAllester (1998).

**Theorem 1** (A PAC-Bayesian theorem) **.** _First, fix a probability measure_ P _over the weight space_ Ω _of a classifier. Let S denote a training set of n datapoints sampled iid from the data distribution D and let VS denote the corresponding solution space. Consider the population error_ 0 _≤ εD_ ( _w_ ) _≤_ 1 _of weight setting w ∈_ Ω _, and its average over the solution space εD_ ( _VS_ ) := E _w∼_ P[ _εD_ ( _w_ ) _| w ∈ VS_ ] _. Then, for a proportion_ 1 _− δ of draws of the training set S,_

For large _n_ , the ln 2 _n/δ_ term is negligible and the result says that the population error averaged over solutions is less than the ratio of the negative log volume of solutions to the number of training points.

**Theorem 2** (Upper bound on the average population error of an infinitely wide neural network) **.** _First, fix a probability measure_ P _over the weight space_ Ω _of an infinitely wide neural network. Let S denote a training set of n datapoints sampled iid from the data distribution D, let y ∈{±_ 1 _}[n] denote the binary vector of training labels, and let VS denote the corresponding solution space. Consider the population error_ 0 _≤ εD_ ( _w_ ) _≤_ 1 _of weight setting w ∈_ Ω _, and its average over the solution space εD_ ( _VS_ ) := E _w∼_ P[ _εD_ ( _w_ ) _| w ∈ VS_ ] _. _where the complexity measures C_ 0 _and C_ 1 _are defined in Lemma 1._

Since the complexity measure _C_ 1 is an analytical function of the NNGP covariance matrix Σ and the binary vector of training labels _y_ , Theorem 2 is an analytical generalisation bound for the NNGP posterior. For large _n_ , the result simplifies to: _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ _C_ 0(Σ _, y_ ) _/n ≤_ 1 _/_ 5 + _C_ 1(Σ _, y_ ) _/n_ . So the bound depends on the ratio of complexity measures _C_ 0 and _C_ 1 to the number of datapoints _n_ .

To gain further intuition about Theorem 2, consider two special cases. First, suppose that the neural architecture induces no correlation between any pair of distinct data points, such that Σ = I. Then _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ _C_ 0(I _, y_ ) _/n_ = ln 2 _≈_ 0 _._ 7 and the bound is worse than chance. This corresponds to _pure memorisation_ of the training labels. Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that Σ _ij_ = _yiyj_ . Although this Σ is singular, it may be seen directly that P[ _VS_ ] = 1 _/_ 2. Then by Theorem 1, _εD_ ( _VS_ ) ⪅ ln 2 _/n_ which is much better than chance for large _n_ . This corresponds to _pure generalisation_ from the training labels. Interpolating between these two extremes would suggest that a good neural architecture would impose a prior on functions with strong intra-class and weak inter-class correlations.

## 4 TESTING THE BOUND

This section compares the generalisation bound for infinite width networks (Theorem 2) to the performance of finite width multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) trained by gradient descent. The bound is found to be non-vacuous and correlated with the effects of varying depth and dataset complexity. Still, there is a substantial gap between the bound and gradient descent, which is investigated in Section 5.

Three modified versions of the MNIST handwritten digit dataset (LeCun et al., 1998) of varying “hardness” were used in the experiments, as detailed in Figure 2 (top left). MLPs were trained with _L_ layers and _W_ hidden units per layer. Specifically, each 28px _×_ 28px input image _x_ was flattened to lie in R[784] , and normalised to satisfy _∥x∥_ 2 = _√_ 784. The networks consisted of an input layer in R[784] _[×][W]_ , ( _L −_ 2) layers in R _[W][ ×][W]_ , and an output layer in R _[W][ ×]_[1] _._ The nonlinearity _ϕ_ was set to _ϕ_ ( _z_ ) := _√_ 2 _·_ max(0 _, z_ ). For this architecture, the width _W →∞_ kernel is the _compositional arccosine kernel_ described in Theorem 4 in Appendix A.3. For the finite width networks, the training loss was set to square loss using the binary training labels as regression targets, and the networks were trained for 100 epochs using the Nero optimiser (Liu et al., 2021) with an initial learning rate of 0.01 decayed by a factor of 0.9 every epoch, and a mini-batch size of min(50 _,_ training set size) data points. The final train error was 0% in all reported experiments. All bounds were computed with a failure probability of _δ_ = 0 _._ 01, and _C_ 0 was estimated using 10[6] Monte-Carlo samples. All experiments were run on one NVIDIA Titan RTX GPU.

The generalisation bound of Theorem 2 was first compared across three datasets of varying complexity. The network architecture was set to a depth _L_ = 7 MLP, and the bound was computed via MonteCarlo estimation of _C_ 0. The results are shown in Figure 2 (top right). The bound was found to reflect the relative hardness of the datasets. For _random labels_ , the bound was vacuous as desired. Next, the generalisation bound was compared against the empirical performance of a depth _L_ = 7 MLP trained on the _decimal digits_ dataset. The results are shown in Figure 2 (bottom left). While loose compared to the holdout error of the finite width network, the bound is still non-vacuous. Finally, the effect of varying network depth was investigated on the _decimal digits_ dataset. Two depths were compared: _L_ = 2 and _L_ = 7. The results are shown in Figure 2 (bottom right). The bounds (computed via Monte-Carlo estimation of _C_ 0) appear to predict the relative holdout performance of the two architectures at finite width. These results corroborate those of Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) but without the use of the statistically inconsistent expectation-propagation approximation.

## 5 IMPLICIT BIAS OF GRADIENT DESCENT

In Section 3, a bound was derived on the average population error of all infinitely wide neural networks that fit a certain training set. Since the bounded quantity measures the performance of all solutions rather than just those returned by gradient descent, it is intended to assess the implicit bias of architecture. In Section 4, this bound was tested and found to be non-vacuous. Still a substantial gap was found between the bound and the performance of finite width networks trained by gradient descent. This gap could arise for several potential reasons:

- i) slackness in the bounding technique;

- ii) a difference between infinite width and finite width neural networks;

- iii) an additional implicit bias of gradient descent.

This section tests these various possibilities, ultimately concluding that gradient descent does have an important additional implicit bias: the ability to control the margin of the returned network.

## 5.1 AN EXACT FORMULA FOR THE AVERAGE HOLDOUT ERROR OF SOLUTIONS

To investigate whether slackness in the bounding technique is responsible for the gap, an additional theoretical tool was developed: a formula for the holdout error of a binary classifier averaged over the solution space. In contrast to Theorem 2 which gives an upper bound on population error, Theorem 3 is an equality and therefore does not suffer from slackness. The proof is given in Appendix A.1.

**Theorem 3** (Average holdout error of a binary classifier) **.** _First, fix a probability measure_ P _over the weight space_ Ω _of a binary classifier. Let S denote a training set and let VS denote the solution space. For a holdout set T of m datapoints, consider the holdout error_ 0 _≤ εT_ ( _w_ ) _≤_ 1 _of weight setting w ∈_ Ω _, and its average over the solution space εT_ ( _VS_ ) := E _w∼_ P[ _εT_ ( _w_ ) _| w ∈ VS_ ] _. Then:_

In words, the average holdout error over solutions equals the reduction in the volume of solutions when the training set is augmented with a negated holdout point, averaged over the holdout set. For an infinitely wide neural network, Equation 11 involves computing a sum of ratios of Gaussian orthant probabilities. These probabilities can be consistently estimated by Equation 8 in Lemma 1.

## 5.2 THREE-WAY COMPARISON OF HOLDOUT ERROR

Armed with Theorem 3, this subsection makes a three-way comparison between the holdout error of infinite width networks averaged over solutions, the holdout error of finite width networks averaged over solutions, and the holdout error of finite width networks trained by gradient descent.

To compute the holdout error of finite width networks averaged over solutions, weight vectors were randomly sampled and all non-solutions were discarded. To make this process computationally tractable, a very small training set was used consisting of only 5 samples from _binary digits_ . A holdout set of 50 datapoints was used, and the network was set to a 7-layer MLP. The results were:

average holdout error at infinite width: 0 _._ 337 _±_ 0 _._ 001; average holdout error at width 10,000: 0 _._ 33 _±_ 0 _._ 01; gradient descent holdout error at width 10,000: 0 _._ 178 _±_ 0 _._ 007 _._

Based on these results, three comments are in order. First, the close agreement between the average holdout error at finite and infinite width suggests that the infinite width limit may not be responsible for the significant gap observed in Section 4. Second, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests slackness in the bounding technique in Theorem 2 may also not be the main culprit. Third, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests that gradient descent _does_ have an extra implicit bias. The next subsection attempts to diagnose this implicit bias.

## 5.3 DIAGNOSIS: MARGIN

This subsection finds that gradient descent has an important implicit bias in determining the margin of the returned network. This conclusion is made by studying the generalisation error of functions along the _label ray_ in function space—depicted in Figure 3 (top left). For a training set _S_ with a vector of binary labels _y ∈{±_ 1 _}[n]_ , a function _α-far_ along the label ray refers to the point _αy ∈FS_ .
For the case of a zero mean NNGP, the predictive distribution on a holdout set _T_ conditioned on training labels _α_ -far along the label ray is given by Bishop (2006, Chapter 2.3.1):

where Σ _T S_ is the covariance between holdout and train inputs, and Σ _ST_ , Σ _SS_ and Σ _T T_ are defined analogously. For large _α_ , predictions are driven by the posterior mean, whereas for small _α_ , they are driven by random fluctuations. So one expects that letting _α →∞_ should improve holdout error.
For the case of gradient descent training, functions _α_ -far along the label ray can be returned by minimising the following loss function:

A subtle but important point is that for gradient descent training with Nero (Liu et al., 2021) the norms of the weight matrices are constrained, thus _α_ controls a properly normalised notion of margin.

An experiment was performed to measure the holdout error of networks _α_ -far along the label ray—for both NNGP draws and neural networks selected by Nero—for _α_ ranging from 10 _[−]_[2] to 10[2] . As can be seen in Figure 3 (top right), varying _α_ appears to directly control the holdout error, despite all solutions attaining 0% train error. Moreover, large _α_ solutions significantly outperform the average holdout error over the solution space. This suggests that gradient descent possesses a significant implicit bias in its ability to control margin—going beyond the implicit bias of architecture.

To sanity check this result, experiments from Section 4 were repeated with the loss function _Lα_ =0 _._ 1 replacing _Lα_ =1. As can be seen in Figure 3 (bottom), the holdout performance was significantly diminshed at _α_ = 0 _._ 1, as was the gap with the PAC-Bayes bound from Theorem 2.

## 6 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

This paper has explored the separate implicit biases of architecture and gradient descent. Section 3 derived an analytical generalisation bound on the NNGP posterior, Section 4 found this bound to be non-vacuous, while Section 5 showed that large margin functions substantially outperform the bound.

The findings in this paper support the importance of the _implicit bias of architecture_ : reproducing the findings in Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) but with improved technical tools, the average generalisation performance of architecture was already found to be good. But the _implicit bias of gradient descent_ was also found to be important. In particular, in contrast to an assumption made by Valle-Pérez & Louis (2020) that gradient descent “samples the zero-error region close to uniformly”, it was found that gradient descent can be used to target _zero-error functions with large margin_ . This also subtly counters a proposal by Mingard et al. (2021) that gradient descent acts like a “Bayesian sampler”. In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin _α →∞_ for which the Bayesian posterior probability _→_ 0. And indeed, these _minimum a posteriori_ functions seem to generalise best.

One direction of future work suggested by these results is an improvement to the function space PAC-Bayes theory to account for margin. While margin-based PAC-Bayes bounds do already exist (Herbrich, 2001; Langford & Shawe-Taylor, 2003; Neyshabur et al., 2018), these bounds operate in weight space and further do not seem to imply an advantage to selecting the max-margin classifier over the other classifiers included in the PAC-Bayes posterior.

Ultimately, a generalisation theory that properly accounts for the various implicit biases of deep learning could provide a more principled basis for both neural architecture design as well as the design of new regularisation schemes. It is hoped that the new analytical results as well as experimental insights included in this paper contribute a step towards reaching that goal.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a compelling case for disentangling the implicit biases of architecture and gradient descent by combining analytical PAC-Bayes bounds with concrete experiments on NNGP and finite-width networks. It advances the state of the art by deriving a tighter, analytically tractable generalization bound for the NNGP posterior and providing empirical evidence that gradient descent selects large-margin solutions beyond what the architecture alone would suggest. The integration of theoretical and experimental components is a strength, particularly in demonstrating that margin plays a key role in generalization even when average posterior performance is already non-trivial.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguous operationalization of solution space geometry in high dimensions: The paper relies on the volume of the solution space $\mathbb{P}[V_S]$ as a central quantity, interpreted via Gaussian orthant probabilities under the NNGP correspondence. However, in high-dimensional function spaces, the notion of 'volume' becomes problematic due to concentration of measure effects—regions of high probability mass may not align with geometric volume as defined by orthant probabilities. The analysis assumes that a larger orthant probability directly implies better generalization, but this ignores the role of curvature and local geometry around solutions. For instance, a solution might lie in a broad but shallow basin that generalizes poorly despite occupying a large volume. The paper does not address whether the derived bounds reflect actual typical-case behavior or are dominated by rare but high-volume configurations.

- Over-reliance on theoretical bounds that may not reflect empirical performance: Theoretical bounds in Theorem 2 depend on $\mathcal{C}_0(\Sigma, y)$ and $\mathcal{C}_1(\Sigma, y)$, which are derived under the NNGP assumption of infinite width and Gaussian-distributed outputs. While the paper validates the bound's qualitative trends in finite-width networks, the quantitative gap remains substantial. This suggests that the bound, while non-vacuous, may not tightly capture the actual generalization behavior of practical networks. Moreover, the bound deteriorates with increasing $n$, yet real-world performance often improves. The paper would benefit from a more critical discussion of the bound's limitations in predicting actual test error, especially given that modern deep networks operate far from the infinite-width regime in terms of feature learning.

- Insufficient validation of gradient descent’s margin bias under realistic optimization dynamics: The paper attributes the performance gap between the NNGP posterior average and gradient descent to an implicit margin bias of GD. However, the experiments use Nero, a variant of Adam with weight norm constraints, rather than vanilla gradient descent. This raises concerns about whether the observed margin control is due to the specific optimizer or a general property of GD. The paper acknowledges Nero's norm control but does not test whether standard SGD or Adam without such constraints exhibits the same behavior. Without this comparison, the claim that gradient descent inherently selects large-margin solutions is not fully substantiated, particularly given prior work suggesting stochasticity can dominate implicit bias.

- Unverified assumption linking flatness and generalization via margin: The paper assumes that large margin implies better generalization and identifies this as the mechanism by which gradient descent improves upon the architecture's bias. While margin is a well-motivated heuristic, the paper does not empirically verify that the observed margin–generalization relationship holds across architectures or datasets. For example, it does not test whether adversarial robustness or calibration improves with margin, nor does it consider whether other function-space properties (e.g., frequency bias) could explain the gains. The assumption that margin is the primary driver of improved generalization remains plausible but unproven within the study’s design.

- Potential mismatch between assumed and actual optimization dynamics: The paper treats gradient descent as a mechanism that can be steered along the 'label ray' via scaling of targets in the loss function (Equation 14). However, in finite-width networks, gradient descent trajectories are path-dependent and influenced by initialization, learning rate schedules, and non-convexity. The experimental setup assumes that minimizing $\mathcal{L}_\alpha$ reliably produces functions $\alpha$-far along the label ray, but this is not verified. In practice, such scaling might lead to poor conditioning or failure to converge. The paper would be strengthened by including learning curves or convergence diagnostics to confirm that the intended solutions are actually reached.

- No computation of average error in a canonical model: The paper claims Theorem 3 enables consistent estimation of the average error of the NNGP posterior, yet it never computes this quantity in a fully specified, analytically tractable model where the result can be verified or calibrated. A natural test case is the linear network with Gaussian inputs, where the NNGP kernel is simply the linear kernel and all quantities—orthant probabilities, posterior means, and average holdout errors—can be computed explicitly. Readers will wonder whether the Monte Carlo estimates in Section 5.2 converge to a known ground truth in such a setting. Compute equation (11) for a two-point dataset with linear architecture and isotropic Gaussian inputs, and verify that the average holdout error matches a direct analytic computation. This would anchor the empirical estimates in a understood case and validate the numerical approach.

- Missing comparison to flat minima literature via explicit sharpness measurement: The paper attributes improved generalization to large margin but does not connect this to the broader literature on flat minima or sharpness, despite the relevance of these concepts to PAC-Bayes and generalization. A standard diagnostic—e.g., the Hessian spectrum or small-noise stability of the solution—is never reported. This omission weakens the claim that margin is the key factor, as sharpness and margin often co-vary. To strengthen the argument, the authors should compute the top eigenvalues of the Hessian at convergence for networks trained with $\mathcal{L}_{\alpha=0.1}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{\alpha=10}$ on the same small dataset used in Section 5.2. If large $\alpha$ solutions are both higher margin and flatter, the margin-flatness link is supported; if not, the mechanism requires re-evaluation.

- No analysis of label ray behavior in non-Gaussian regimes: The label ray analysis in Section 5.3 assumes the NNGP posterior remains Gaussian when conditioning on $\alpha y$, which holds only in the infinite-width limit with fixed features. However, in finite-width networks, increasing $\alpha$ may induce feature learning, breaking the Gaussianity assumption. The paper does not test whether the observed generalization gains along the label ray persist when feature adaptation occurs. This is a notable gap because if performance improves only in the lazy regime, the findings do not generalize to practical deep learning. The authors should re-run the $\alpha$-scaling experiment on a narrow network (e.g., width 50) where feature learning is active, and compare the holdout error trend to the infinite-width case. This would clarify the scope of the margin bias claim.

- Unverified assumption that $\mathcal{C}_0$ estimates are consistent at large $n$: The paper relies on Monte Carlo estimation of $\mathcal{C}_0(\Sigma, y)$ via Lemma 1, but does not assess the estimator's convergence as $n$ increases. For large datasets, the Gaussian orthant probability becomes extremely small, and the expectation in equation (8) may have high variance, making estimation unreliable. This threatens the validity of the bound in Figure 2 when $n > 1000$. The authors should plot the standard error of $\mathcal{C}_0$ estimates as a function of $n$ for the decimal digits dataset and report effective sample size. If the estimates become unstable, the claimed non-vacuousness of the bound at large $n$ may be an artifact of poor sampling.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the interpretation of solution space volume in high dimensions, addressing potential pitfalls due to concentration of measure and distinguishing between volume and typicality.

- Provide a more critical analysis of the tightness and predictive power of the PAC-Bayes bound in finite-width settings, possibly including ablation studies on network width.

- Include a comparison of margin selection behavior across optimizers (e.g., SGD, Adam, Nero) to isolate whether the observed bias is due to gradient descent or specific implementation choices.

- Add empirical validation that large-margin solutions generalize better not just in accuracy but in robustness or calibration, to strengthen the causal link between margin and generalization.

- Verify experimentally that minimizing $\mathcal{L}_\alpha$ successfully produces functions at the intended margin, including convergence diagnostics and sensitivity analysis.

comments.title: Misuse of 'minimum a posteriori' terminology
comments.quote: This highlights a curious fact: *minimum a posteriori* functions can generalise best, and gradient descent can select for those functions.
comments.explanation: The term 'minimum a posteriori' is not standard and is misleading. In Bayesian inference, 'a posteriori' refers to the posterior distribution, and 'maximum a posteriori' (MAP) denotes the most probable function. The paper intends to describe functions with *vanishingly small* posterior probability under the NNGP—precisely the opposite of MAP. While these functions are rare, calling them 'minimum a posteriori' incorrectly suggests a formal optimization objective. The intended point—that gradient descent selects atypical, high-margin solutions—is valid, but the terminology risks confusion and should be replaced with 'least probable under the posterior' or similar.

comments.title: Invalid application of Theorem 2 to singular covariance
comments.quote: Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that $\Sigma_{ij}=y_{i}y_{j}$. Although this $\Sigma$ is singular, it may be seen directly that $\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]=1/2$. Then by Theorem 1, $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})\lessapprox\ln 2/n$ which is much better than chance for large $n$.
comments.explanation: The example assumes $\Sigma = y y^T$, which is rank-one and singular. However, Lemma 1 and Theorem 2 rely on $\Sigma^{-1}$ and $\det \Sigma$, both undefined for singular matrices. Thus, $\mathcal{C}_0(\Sigma,y)$ and $\mathcal{C}_1(\Sigma,y)$ are not valid, and the bound in Theorem 2 cannot be applied. While the conclusion $\mathbb{P}[V_S] = 1/2$ is correct, the justification via Theorem 2 is formally incorrect. The argument should be revised using a regularized $\Sigma_\epsilon = y y^T + \epsilon \mathbb{I}$ and taking $\epsilon \to 0$, ensuring consistency with the theorem's assumptions.

comments.title: Incorrect PAC-Bayes inequality chain
comments.quote:
Then, for a proportion $1-\delta$ of draws of the training set $S$,

$\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})\leq\ln\frac{1}{1-\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})}\leq\frac{\ln\frac{1}{\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]}+\ln\frac{2n}{\delta}}{n-1}.$ (4)
comments.explanation: The double inequality misrepresents the logic of PAC-Bayes bounds. While $\varepsilon \leq \ln(1/(1-\varepsilon))$ holds, presenting it as part of a bounding chain suggests the final expression bounds $\varepsilon$ directly, which it does not. The correct derivation from the KL divergence yields $\varepsilon \leq 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\ln(1/\mathbb{P}[V_S]) + \ln(2n/\delta)}{n-1}\right)$. The current form incorrectly implies $\varepsilon$ is bounded above by a larger quantity, obscuring the actual dependence. Equation (4) should be rewritten to reflect the proper inversion of the KL bound.

comments.title: Unjustified lower bound in Lemma 1
comments.quote: p\geq\frac{1}{2^{n}}\mathrm{e}^{\frac{n}{2}-\sqrt[n]{\det\Sigma}\left[(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{\pi})\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma^{-1})+\frac{1}{\pi}y^{T}\Sigma^{-1}y\right]}=:\frac{1}{2^{n}}\mathrm{e}^{\frac{n}{2}-\mathcal{C}_{1}(\Sigma,y)}.
comments.explanation: The lower bound (9) is stated without justification. The coefficients $1/2 - 1/\pi$ and $1/\pi$ appear ad hoc, and no derivation or reference is provided. While the bound is tight for $\Sigma = \mathbb{I}$, its validity for general $\Sigma \succ 0$ and all $y \in \{\pm1\}^n$ is not established. Such bounds typically rely on Gaussian hypercontractivity or Slepian-type inequalities, but none are cited. The authors must either derive the bound or reference a known result to ensure its correctness.

comments.title: Missing justification for Monte Carlo estimator unbiasedness
comments.quote: Equation 8 yields an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator of Gaussian orthant probabilities, and Inequality 9 yields a lower bound.
comments.explanation: The claim that Equation (8) yields an unbiased estimator is stated without proof or reference. The transformation $u \mapsto y \odot |u|$ maps a standard Gaussian to the positive orthant, but the quadratic form involving $\sqrt[n]{\det\Sigma}\Sigma^{-1} - \mathbb{I}$ lacks justification. To verify unbiasedness, one must show the expectation recovers the orthant integral under $\mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma)$. Without a derivation or citation (e.g., to Shepp's formula or Gaussian correlation inequalities), the estimator's validity is not established. A sketch or reference should be added.

comments.title: Volume ratio assumes subset condition not generally valid
comments.quote:
Then:

$\varepsilon_{T}(V_{S})=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{(x,y)\in T}\frac{\mathbb{P}[V_{S\cup(x,-y)}]}{\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]}.$ (11)

In words, the average holdout error over solutions equals the reduction in the volume of solutions when the training set is augmented with a negated holdout point, averaged over the holdout set.
comments.explanation: The expression assumes $V_{S\cup(x,-y)} \subseteq V_S$, so that the ratio $\mathbb{P}[V_{S\cup(x,-y)}]/\mathbb{P}[V_S]$ equals the conditional probability of misclassification. But in general, adding $(x,-y)$ may introduce solutions that violate constraints in $S$, so the inclusion does not hold. The correct expression is $\varepsilon_T(V_S) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{(x,y)\in T} \mathbb{P}[f_w(x) = -y \mid w \in V_S]$, which cannot be reduced to a volume ratio without additional assumptions. The current formulation incorrectly conflates set containment with probabilistic conditioning.

comments.title: Ambiguous reuse of measure symbol without clarification
comments.quote:
The measure  $\mathbb{P}$  on weight space induces a measure  $\mathbb{P}$  on function space via the relation:

$$
\mathbb {P} [ F \subset \mathcal {F} _ {S} ] := \mathbb {P} \left\{w \in \Omega \mid \left(f \left(x ^ {(1)}, w\right), \dots , f \left(x ^ {(n)}, w\right)\right) \in F \right\}. \tag {3}
$$
comments.explanation: Using $\mathbb{P}$ for both weight and function space measures creates ambiguity. The induced measure is the pushforward under the map $w \mapsto f(x,w)$, which may not preserve volume due to non-injectivity (e.g., symmetries in neural networks). Without distinguishing $\mathbb{P}_f$, readers may assume uniform weighting over $\mathcal{F}_S$, which is incorrect. A precise treatment should define the pushforward explicitly to avoid confusion in probabilistic statements.

comments.title: Failure to address non-injectivity in function space mapping
comments.quote:
The function space  $\mathcal{F}_S$  is defined to be the set of outputs that the classifier  $f$  can realise on dataset  $S$ :

$$
\mathcal {F} _ {S} := \left\{\left(f \left(x ^ {(1)}, w\right), \dots , f \left(x ^ {(n)}, w\right)\right) \mid w \in \Omega \right\} \subset \mathbb {R} ^ {n}. \tag {2}
$$
comments.explanation: The definition of $\mathcal{F}_S$ ignores how the induced measure $\mathbb{P}_f$ weights function vectors by the volume of their preimages in $\Omega$. In overparameterized models, symmetries (e.g., neuron permutations) mean some function vectors have larger preimage volumes. The paper assumes a direct correspondence between function space volume and generalization without justifying how the geometry of the parameter-to-function map affects $\mathbb{P}[V_S]$. This gap undermines the interpretation of solution volume as a meaningful complexity measure.

comments.title: Unverified scaling of network outputs with $\alpha$
comments.quote:
For the case of gradient descent training, functions $\alpha$-far along the label ray can be returned by minimising the following loss function:

$\mathcal{L}_{\alpha}(W):=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(f(x^{(i)};W)-\alpha y^{(i)}\right)^{2}.$ (14)
comments.explanation: The paper assumes minimizing $\mathcal{L}_\alpha$ produces outputs scaling as $\alpha y$, placing solutions at $\alpha y \in \mathcal{F}_S$. But in finite-width networks outside the lazy regime, the parameter-function map is nonlinear, and output scaling may not track $\alpha$ due to feature learning. Without empirical verification—such as reporting output norms across $\alpha$—this assumption is unsubstantiated. For the 7-layer MLP used, proportionality is not guaranteed. The authors should confirm output scaling in experiments or clarify the regime of validity.

comments.title: No verification that solutions are in the same orthant
comments.quote: While all functions in the grey shaded orthant attain zero train error, the darker shaded solutions have small margin and thus—under a Gaussian process model—holdout predictions are expected to be driven by random fluctuations.
comments.explanation: The analysis assumes all solutions lie in the correct orthant ($\text{sign}(f(x^{(i)})) = y^{(i)}$), but minimizing $\mathcal{L}_\alpha$ with large $\alpha$ does not guarantee correct signs on training points, especially during optimization. If some solutions violate the orthant condition, they do not achieve zero train error and should be excluded. The paper does not report whether zero train error was verified in the $\alpha$-sweep experiments. Without this check, the interpretation of $\alpha$-scaling within the solution space is invalid.

comments.title: Nero-specific norm constraints misattributed to gradient descent
comments.quote: A subtle but important point is that for gradient descent training with Nero *(Liu et al., 2021)* the norms of the weight matrices are constrained, thus $\alpha$ controls a properly normalised notion of margin.
comments.explanation: The interpretation of $\alpha$ as controlling normalized margin relies on Nero's explicit weight norm constraints. Standard gradient descent does not enforce such constraints, so scaling targets by $\alpha$ does not necessarily increase margin in a controlled way. The paper attributes this behavior to gradient descent broadly, but the mechanism is specific to Nero. This overgeneralizes an optimizer-specific effect. The text should clarify that margin control depends on Nero's constraints and that the findings may not extend to vanilla GD.

comments.title: Overstatement of gradient descent targeting margin $\to\infty$
comments.quote: In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin $\alpha\to\infty$ for which the Bayesian posterior probability $\to 0$. And indeed, these *minimum a posteriori* functions seem to generalise best.
comments.explanation: The claim that gradient descent can target $\alpha \to \infty$ is unjustified. As $\alpha$ increases, the function $\alpha y$ becomes unbounded, and in practice, optimization may fail to converge or suffer numerical instability. The paper's experiments use finite $\alpha$, and no evidence shows performance improves monotonically as $\alpha \to \infty$. The posterior probability tending to zero does not imply such functions are reachable. The statement should be revised to describe movement along the label ray for finite $\alpha$, not an infinite limit.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of function space solution set as orthant
comments.quote: in function space the solution space of a binary classification task is just an orthant.
comments.explanation: The solution space is not a coordinate orthant unless labels align with the standard basis. For general $y$, it is the signed orthant $\{ f \in \mathbb{R}^n : \text{sign}(f_i) = y_i \}$, which depends on label alignment. The current phrasing risks misleading readers into thinking the solution space is always the positive orthant, which would invalidate the label-dependent terms in $\mathcal{C}_0(\Sigma, y)$. The sentence should clarify that the solution space is the orthant defined by the sign pattern of $y$.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'failure probability' without distributional context
comments.quote: All bounds were computed with a failure probability of $\delta=0.01$, and $\mathcal{C}_{0}$ was estimated using $10^{6}$ Monte-Carlo samples.
comments.explanation: The term 'failure probability' is misleading. Theorem 2 provides a deterministic bound given $S$; there is no probabilistic failure mode. The $\delta$ parameter appears to be a misapplication of PAC-Bayes notation where the bound holds with probability $1-\delta$ over $S$, but the theorem is stated conditionally on $S$. If $\delta$ refers to Monte Carlo estimation uncertainty, that should be specified. Otherwise, the phrase should be removed or clarified.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a nuanced view on neural network generalization, arguing that both architectural bias and gradient descent's margin-seeking bias contribute. While the technical contributions are novel, there are significant concerns about the experimental validation of theoretical claims and the scope of the conclusions drawn.

The paper makes valuable theoretical contributions with its analytical PAC-Bayes bound and exact formula for average holdout error. The technical derivations appear sound and provide useful tools for studying implicit bias. However, the empirical validation is limited in scope and may not fully support the strong claims about gradient descent's margin bias being the primary explanation for the observed performance gap.

Overview issues:

- Limited experimental validation of margin bias claim: The paper's central claim that gradient descent's margin bias explains the performance gap relies heavily on a single small-scale experiment (5 training points) in Section 5.2. While the three-way comparison shows differences between GD and random sampling, this tiny dataset may not reflect behavior in realistic settings. The label ray experiments in Section 5.3 are more convincing but still use simplified loss functions rather than standard training. To substantiate the margin bias claim, the paper needs experiments showing: (a) GD naturally finds large-margin solutions without the specialized L_α loss on standard datasets, and (b) the correlation between margin size and generalization holds across multiple architectures and dataset sizes.

- Questionable generalization from infinite-width to finite-width networks: The paper draws strong conclusions about finite-width networks based primarily on infinite-width analysis (Theorem 2) and NNGP posterior calculations. While Section 5.2 shows some agreement between width 10,000 and infinite width on a tiny dataset, this is insufficient evidence that the infinite-width results meaningfully predict behavior in practical networks. The literature context notes that finite-width effects can be significant, yet the paper doesn't investigate how width interacts with the claimed margin bias. The authors should either temper their claims about finite-width networks or provide systematic analysis of how the gap between infinite and finite width varies with width, depth, and dataset size.

- Insufficient analysis of alternative explanations for the performance gap: The paper dismisses two alternative explanations for the gap between Theorem 2's bound and GD performance: (1) slackness in the bound, and (2) finite-width effects. The evidence against slackness comes only from the exact formula in Theorem 3 applied to a tiny dataset. More critically, the paper doesn't seriously consider other potential implicit biases of GD beyond margin, such as implicit regularization toward flat minima or specific spectral properties. The literature context mentions several open questions about GD's bias that remain unaddressed. A more thorough analysis would quantify how much of the gap is explained by margin versus other factors.

- Weak connection to practical neural network training: The experiments use square loss with binary labels as regression targets, which is non-standard for classification. More importantly, the specialized L_α loss function used to probe margin bias (Equation 14) doesn't reflect how neural networks are typically trained. The Nero optimizer with constrained weight norms is also not standard practice. While these choices may be necessary for theoretical analysis, they raise questions about whether the observed margin bias phenomenon would occur under standard training regimes (e.g., cross-entropy loss with SGD/Adam). The paper should include experiments with standard training setups to demonstrate the relevance of their findings.

- Inadequate discussion of limitations and scope: The paper overstates the generality of its conclusions without acknowledging important limitations. For instance: (1) The analysis is restricted to binary classification with specific network architectures (MLPs with ReLU); (2) The datasets used are simplified variants of MNIST; (3) The theoretical results assume zero training error, which may not hold in noisy or non-separable settings. The literature context mentions that GD's behavior under label noise and in non-separable cases remains an open question, yet the paper doesn't address how its findings might extend to these more realistic scenarios. A more balanced discussion would explicitly state these limitations.

- Missing worked example of bound calculation: The paper derives an analytical PAC-Bayes bound in Theorem 2 but never computes it for a concrete, fully-specified model. Readers need to see the bound evaluated step-by-step for a simple architecture to understand how the complexity measures C₀ and C₁ behave. The paper should compute the bound for a two-layer linear network or a shallow ReLU network on a synthetic dataset, showing how the covariance matrix Σ is constructed and how the resulting bound depends on architectural hyperparameters like width and depth. This would help calibrate intuitions about when the bound is tight versus loose.

- No demonstration of margin bias in standard training: The paper's key claim about gradient descent's margin bias relies on the specialized L_α loss function rather than standard training. To establish that this phenomenon occurs in practice, the paper needs to show that networks trained with standard cross-entropy loss and SGD/Adam naturally converge to large-margin solutions on realistic datasets. The authors should measure the margin of standardly-trained networks and correlate it with generalization performance, demonstrating that the margin bias emerges without artificial constraints. This is crucial for connecting the theoretical insights to actual deep learning practice.

- Missing analysis of width-dependent effects: While the paper briefly compares infinite and finite width on a tiny dataset, it lacks systematic analysis of how the gap between infinite-width theory and finite-width practice varies with network width. The authors should study how the bound's tightness and the margin bias phenomenon change across a range of widths (e.g., 100, 1000, 10000) on standard datasets. This would help readers understand at what widths the infinite-width approximation becomes reasonable and whether the claimed effects persist in the more common regime of moderately-sized networks.

- No implementation guidance for bound computation: The paper presents an analytical bound but provides no practical guidance for researchers who want to compute it for their own architectures. While Monte Carlo estimation of C₀ is mentioned, the paper doesn't discuss computational costs, numerical stability issues, or convergence rates. The authors should include implementation details such as recommended number of samples, handling of near-singular covariance matrices, and computational complexity. This would make the theoretical contribution more useful for practitioners seeking to apply these bounds to new architectures.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add experiments with standard training setups (cross-entropy loss, SGD/Adam) on larger datasets to demonstrate that the margin bias phenomenon occurs in practice

- Systematically analyze how the gap between infinite-width theory and finite-width practice varies with network width, depth, and dataset size

- Investigate and quantify alternative explanations for the performance gap beyond margin bias

- Include experiments showing GD naturally finds large-margin solutions without the specialized L_α loss function

- Temper claims and add explicit discussion of the limitations (binary classification only, zero training error assumption, specific architectures)

comments.title: Incorrect inequality direction in Theorem 1
comments.quote: $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})\leq\ln\frac{1}{1-\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})}\leq\frac{\ln\frac{1}{\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]}+\ln\frac{2n}{\delta}}{n-1}.$ (4)
comments.explanation: The first inequality in Theorem 1 is incorrect. For $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) \in (0,1)$, we have $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) > \ln\frac{1}{1-\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})}$ because the function $f(x) = \ln\frac{1}{1-x}$ is convex and lies below the line $y=x$ for $x \in (0,1)$. For example, at $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) = 0.5$, we have $0.5 > \ln(2) \approx 0.693$. The correct inequality should be $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) \geq \ln\frac{1}{1-\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})}$. Rewrite the inequality as $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) \geq \ln\frac{1}{1-\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})} \leq \frac{\ln\frac{1}{\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]}+\ln\frac{2n}{\delta}}{n-1}$ because the standard PAC-Bayes bound uses the convexity of the log function.

comments.title: Undefined parameter x in Lemma 1 inequality
comments.quote: $p\geq\frac{1}{2^{n}}\mathrm{e}^{\frac{n}{2}-\sqrt[n]{\det\Sigma}\left[(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{x})\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma^{-1})+\frac{1}{x}y^{T}\Sigma^{-1}y\right]}=:\frac{1}{2^{n}}\mathrm{e}^{\frac{n}{2}-\mathcal{C}_{1}(\Sigma,y)}.$
comments.explanation: The parameter x appears in the inequality but is never defined in Lemma 1 or the surrounding text. The expression contains terms like (1/2 - 1/x) and (1/x) that depend on x, but there is no specification of what x represents or what constraints it must satisfy. This makes the bound ill-defined as stated. Rewrite the inequality by defining x as a positive constant parameter or replacing x with its intended value because the current expression is mathematically incomplete.

comments.title: Incorrect simplification in Theorem 2
comments.quote: For large $n$, the result simplifies to: $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S})\lessapprox\mathcal{C}_{0}(\Sigma,y)/n\leq 1/5+\mathcal{C}_{1}(\Sigma,y)/n$.
comments.explanation: From Equation 10, the bound is $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) \leq \frac{\mathcal{C}_{0}(\Sigma,y) + \ln\frac{2n}{\delta}}{n-1}$. For large n, this becomes approximately $\frac{\mathcal{C}_{0}(\Sigma,y)}{n} + \frac{\ln\frac{2n}{\delta}}{n}$, not $\frac{\mathcal{C}_{0}(\Sigma,y)}{n} \leq 1/5 + \frac{\mathcal{C}_{1}(\Sigma,y)}{n}$. The constant 1/5 appears without justification, and the inequality direction between the $\mathcal{C}_{0}$ and $\mathcal{C}_{1}$ terms is reversed from what appears in Equation 10. Rewrite the simplification as $\varepsilon_{D}(V_{S}) \lessapprox \frac{\mathcal{C}_{0}(\Sigma,y)}{n} + \frac{\ln\frac{2n}{\delta}}{n}$ because the original statement incorrectly combines terms and introduces an unjustified constant.

comments.title: Figure 1 caption misrepresents solution space geometry
comments.quote: Figure 1: The solution space  $V_{S}$  of a learning task denotes the subset of classifiers that attain zero error on a dataset  $S$ . While the solution space can have a complicated geometry when described in weight space, in function space the solution space of a binary classification task is just an orthant.
comments.explanation: The caption incorrectly states that the solution space in function space is 'just an orthant' for binary classification. This is only true under the restrictive assumption that the network outputs are unthresholded real-valued functions and we consider the space of all possible functions that achieve zero training error. However, neural networks with fixed architecture induce a specific manifold in function space, and the intersection of this manifold with the zero-error region is generally not the full orthant but a proper subset. The caption should clarify that the orthant represents the space of all possible zero-error functions, but the actual solution space accessible to a given network architecture is a constrained subset within this orthant. Rewrite as: 'Figure 1: The solution space $V_S$ of a learning task denotes the subset of classifiers that attain zero error on a dataset $S$. While the solution space can have a complicated geometry when described in weight space, in function space the space of all possible zero-error binary classifiers forms an orthant. However, the solution space accessible to a fixed network architecture is a constrained subset within this orthant.'

comments.title: Contradictory interpretation of results in Section 5.2
comments.quote: Second, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests slackness in the bounding technique in Theorem 2 may also not be the main culprit.
comments.explanation: This reasoning is logically flawed. Theorem 2 bounds the average population error, not GD performance. The fact that GD (0.178±0.007) outperforms the average (0.33±0.01) tells us nothing about whether Theorem 2's bound is tight. To assess slackness, compare Theorem 2's actual bound value to the true average error. Remove this claim or replace it with: 'Second, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests that GD's implicit bias is substantial, though this does not directly inform us about the tightness of Theorem 2's bound.'

comments.title: Overstated margin bias claim in conclusion
comments.quote: In particular, in contrast to an assumption made by *Valle-Pérez and Louis (2020)* that gradient descent “samples the zero-error region close to uniformly”, it was found that gradient descent can be used to target *zero-error functions with large margin*. This also subtly counters a proposal by *Mingard et al. (2021)* that gradient descent acts like a “Bayesian sampler”. In particular, gradient descent can target functions with margin $\alpha\to\infty$ for which the Bayesian posterior probability $\to 0$. And indeed, these *minimum a posteriori* functions seem to generalise best.
comments.explanation:
The claim that gradient descent can target functions with margin α→∞ is not supported by the experimental evidence in the paper. The experiments in Section 5.2 only show margin effects when using a specialized L_α loss function with α=0.1, not under standard training conditions. The paper never demonstrates that standard gradient descent naturally finds solutions with arbitrarily large margins. The evidence for margin bias comes from artificial experiments with modified loss functions, not from observing this behavior in typical training scenarios. The claim overgeneralizes from limited experimental setups to make a broad statement about gradient descent's capabilities.

Rewrite the passage as: "In particular, in contrast to an assumption made by *Valle-Pérez and Louis (2020)* that gradient descent 'samples the zero-error region close to uniformly', it was found that gradient descent can be biased toward functions with larger margin, especially when using margin-sensitive loss functions. This also subtly counters a proposal by *Mingard et al. (2021)* that gradient descent acts like a 'Bayesian sampler'. In particular, gradient descent can preferentially select functions with larger margins for which the Bayesian posterior probability may be lower. And indeed, these functions with larger margins seem to generalise better in our experiments."

comments.title: Singular covariance matrix case lacks justification
comments.quote: Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that $\Sigma_{ij}=y_{i}y_{j}$. Although this $\Sigma$ is singular, it may be seen directly that $\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]=1/2$.
comments.explanation: When $\Sigma$ is singular, the Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma)$ is degenerate and does not have a proper density. The orthant probability $\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]$ is not well-defined in the standard sense for a singular covariance matrix. The claim that it can be 'seen directly' that $\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]=1/2$ requires justification using limiting behavior of non-singular approximations or a proper definition of Gaussian orthant probabilities for singular covariances. Add a justification showing how $\mathbb{P}[V_{S}]=1/2$ is obtained as a limit of non-singular covariance matrices or cite a reference for singular Gaussian orthant probabilities because the current statement is mathematically incomplete.

comments.title: Missing justification for Monte Carlo estimator unbiasedness
comments.quote: Equation 8 yields an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator of Gaussian orthant probabilities, and Inequality 9 yields a lower bound.
comments.explanation: The claim that Equation 8 yields an unbiased Monte Carlo estimator is stated without proof. To show unbiasedness, one must verify that $\mathbb{E}_{u\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\mathbb{I})}[\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{1}{2}(y\odot|u|)^{T}(\sqrt[n]{\det\Sigma}\Sigma^{-1}-\mathbb{I})(y\odot|u|)}] = 2^n p$, where $p$ is the orthant probability. This requires a derivation showing the equivalence between the expectation and the orthant probability, which is not provided. Add a derivation or reference proving the unbiasedness of the estimator because the claim is currently unsupported.

comments.title: Inconsistent classifier notation
comments.quote:
A classifier  $f$  shall depend on a weight vector  $w\in \Omega$ , where  $\Omega$  denotes the weight space. In particular, for an input  $x$  the prediction is given by sign  $f(x;w)$ .

One is interested in the relationship between the train error  $\varepsilon_{S}$  of a classifier on a dataset  $S$  to the population error  $\varepsilon_{D}$  on a data distribution  $D$ . For a weight vector  $w \in \Omega$ , these are defined as:

$$
\varepsilon_ {S} (w) := \frac {1}{n} \sum_ {i = 1} ^ {n} \mathbb {I} \left[ \operatorname {s i g n} f \left(x ^ {(i)}, w\right) \neq y ^ {(i)} \right]; \quad \varepsilon_ {D} (w) := \mathbb {P} _ {(x, y) \sim D} [ \operatorname {s i g n} f (x; w) \neq y ]. \tag {1}
$$
comments.explanation: The notation inconsistency between $f(x;w)$ in the text and $f(x^{(i)}, w)$ in Equation (1) creates ambiguity about the classifier's output. The text states predictions are given by 'sign $f(x;w)$' but Equation (1) applies sign to $f$ again. Rewrite the classifier definition as 'for an input $x$, the prediction is given by $f(x;w)$' and clarify whether $f$ outputs signed values. Then rewrite Equation (1) as $\varepsilon_{S}(w) := \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{I}[f(x^{(i)}; w) \neq y^{(i)}]$ to maintain consistent semicolon notation throughout.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset description in table
comments.quote:
|  MNIST Variant | Inputs | Labels  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  random labels | {0,1,...,9} | coin flip  |
|  decimal digits | {0,1,...,9} | parity  |
|  binary digits | {0,1} | parity  |
comments.explanation: The table incorrectly lists abstract digit classes as inputs, contradicting the text which states that experiments use 28×28 MNIST images flattened to R^784. The table should be corrected to show that all three variants use MNIST images as inputs with different labeling schemes. Rewrite the table as: | MNIST Variant | Inputs | Labels | | --- | --- | --- | | random labels | 28×28 MNIST images | coin flip | | decimal digits | 28×28 MNIST images | parity of digit | | binary digits | 28×28 MNIST images (0,1 only) | parity of digit | because the current table misrepresents the experimental setup.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper investigates the relative contributions of neural network architecture and gradient descent to generalization by analyzing the NNGP posterior and comparing it to solutions found by gradient descent. It introduces analytical bounds for the NNGP posterior and demonstrates that while architecture provides a baseline bias, gradient descent actively selects for large-margin solutions that outperform the posterior average.

The paper makes a clear, well-motivated contribution to the understanding of generalization by cleanly separating architectural priors from optimization-driven bias. The use of exact formulas for holdout error and the diagnosis of margin-based selection are particularly insightful and well-supported by the provided experiments.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in the definition of margin for finite-width networks: In Section 5.3, the authors use the parameter alpha to control the margin by minimizing the loss in Equation 14. However, the paper does not explicitly define how this alpha relates to the geometric margin in the function space for finite-width networks, especially given that the weights are constrained by the Nero optimizer. Without a formal connection between the loss target alpha and the margin (e.g., the distance to the decision boundary), it is difficult to verify if the observed improvement in generalization is purely due to margin maximization or other properties of the optimization trajectory. The authors should provide a concrete definition of the margin measure used for finite-width networks and demonstrate how it scales with alpha.

- Limitations of the label ray assumption: The analysis in Section 5.3 relies on the 'label ray' in function space, which assumes that the optimal solutions lie along the vector defined by the training labels. This is a strong, simplifying assumption that may not hold for more complex, non-binary, or multi-class datasets where the decision boundary is not simply a scaled version of the training labels. While this approach is useful for a controlled diagnosis, the paper should address whether this 'label ray' bias is a general feature of gradient descent or a consequence of the specific loss function and task structure. Including a brief discussion or a small-scale experiment on a more diverse or non-linear classification task would strengthen the claim that this is a general phenomenon.

- Justification of the infinite-width limit for finite-width phenomena: The paper uses the NNGP correspondence (Theorem 4) to derive analytical bounds, but the core claim is that these bounds explain the behavior of finite-width networks. Section 5.2 shows a comparison between infinite-width and finite-width holdout errors, but the gap in performance remains significant. The paper attributes this mostly to the implicit bias of gradient descent, yet it does not fully rule out the possibility that finite-width effects (e.g., feature learning, non-Gaussianity of the output distribution) contribute to this gap. A more rigorous acknowledgment of where the NNGP approximation breaks down for the architectures tested would improve the theoretical clarity.

- Sensitivity of the Monte Carlo estimator for C0: The complexity measure C0 in Lemma 1 is estimated using Monte Carlo sampling. In Section 4, the authors mention using 10^6 samples, but they do not provide a sensitivity analysis or error bars for this estimator relative to the number of training points. Given that C0 is used to compute the generalization bound, the variance of this estimator could significantly affect the tightness of the bound, especially as the number of training points increases or the covariance matrix becomes ill-conditioned. Adding a brief analysis of the convergence of this estimator or its robustness would increase confidence in the reported bounds.

- Missing empirical verification of the 'large margin' claim for non-binary tasks: The paper relies heavily on the 'label ray' construction to demonstrate that gradient descent targets large-margin functions. This construction is inherently tied to binary classification and the specific geometry of the orthant. To substantiate the claim that this mechanism is a general implicit bias of gradient descent, the authors should perform a simulation on a multi-class dataset (e.g., a subset of CIFAR-10) using a standard loss function like cross-entropy. They should explicitly calculate the margin (e.g., the minimum distance to the decision boundary) for these networks and show that it correlates with test performance, rather than relying solely on the alpha-scaling parameter in the binary label ray setting.

- Lack of guidance on the feasibility of the Theorem 3 estimator: Theorem 3 provides an exact formula for the average holdout error, but its practical utility depends on the ratio of two Gaussian orthant probabilities. As the number of training points $n$ increases, the volume of the solution space $\mathbb{P}[V_S]$ can become extremely small, potentially leading to numerical instability or high variance in the Monte Carlo estimation of this ratio. The paper should include a discussion or a small-scale experiment demonstrating the stability of this estimator as $n$ grows, or provide a threshold where the estimation becomes unreliable. Without this, the claim that Theorem 3 is a 'theoretical tool to enable consistent estimation' remains underdeveloped for real-world applications.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal definition of the margin measure for finite-width networks and show its relationship to the alpha parameter.

- Include a brief discussion on the limitations of the label ray assumption and its potential impact on generalizability to non-binary or multi-class datasets.

- Add a sensitivity analysis for the Monte Carlo estimator of C0 to demonstrate the stability of the reported generalization bounds.

- Clarify the extent to which finite-width effects (beyond gradient descent bias) contribute to the gap between the NNGP posterior and the empirical performance.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in the interpretation of the holdout error gap
comments.quote: nt gap observed in Section 4. Second, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests slackness in the bounding technique in Theorem 2 may also not be the main culprit. Third, the significant gap between the gradient descent holdout error and the holdout error averaged over solutions suggests that gradient descent *does* have an extra implicit bias. The n
comments.explanation: The authors argue that the gap between the Theorem 2 bound and gradient descent performance is not due to slackness in the bound because the average holdout error is higher than the GD holdout error. This logic is flawed. If the bound is derived for the average over the solution space, and the average is significantly worse than the specific solutions found by GD, then the bound is inherently loose for the specific subset of functions GD selects. The fact that GD outperforms the average does not eliminate slackness; it demonstrates that the bound is not tight for the specific inductive bias of GD. Please rephrase this to acknowledge that the bound is loose for the specific solutions found by GD.

comments.title: Geometric inaccuracy regarding function space solution sets
comments.quote: While the solution space can have a complicated geometry when described in weight space, in function space the solution space of a binary classification task is just an orthant.
comments.explanation: The assertion that the solution space is just an orthant in function space is overly restrictive. For a binary classification task, the zero-error condition defines the set {f in R^n : sgn(f_i) = y_i}, which is an orthant. However, for a neural network, the function space is the image of the weight space under the map Phi: W -> R^n. The intersection of this image with the orthant is generally not an orthant itself, as the image Phi(W) is a non-linear manifold. Please clarify that this holds for the target labels in the absence of architectural constraints, or specify that it refers to the target space rather than the reachable function space.

comments.title: Singularity of the covariance matrix in special cases
comments.quote: Next, suppose that the neural architecture induces strong intra-class correlations and strong inter-class anti-correlations, such that $\Sigma_{ij}=y_{i}y_{j}$. Although this $\Sigma$ is singular, it may be seen directly that $\mathb
comments.explanation: The proof relies on Lemma 1, which involves the term Sigma^-1. If Sigma is singular (as in the case Sigma_ij = y_i y_j, which has rank 1 for n > 1), Sigma^-1 is undefined. The authors claim the result holds by inspection, but the complexity measures C0 and C1 defined in Lemma 1 are not well-defined for singular matrices. Please clarify how the bound behaves in the limit as Sigma approaches this singular configuration or provide a regularized version of the bound that remains valid for singular covariance matrices.

comments.title: Inconsistency in analytical classification of Theorem 2
comments.quote: Unlike this paper’s purely analytical bound in Theorem 2, these non-vacuous bounds are all evaluated by iterative computational procedures.
comments.explanation: Labeling the bound in Theorem 2 purely analytical in contrast to other bounds that use iterative computational procedures creates a false dichotomy. While the bound has a closed-form expression, its numerical evaluation relies on Monte-Carlo estimation, which is inherently iterative and stochastic. Please clarify that while the bound has a closed-form expression, its evaluation is subject to the same computational constraints as the methods it contrasts itself against.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a meaningful contribution by disentangling the implicit biases of architecture and gradient descent, introducing analytical tools for bounding and estimating NNGP posterior generalization, and providing experimental evidence that gradient descent selects large-margin solutions. However, several issues weaken the paper's claims, particularly regarding the assumptions underlying the theoretical bounds, the limited empirical validation, and the framing of the contribution relative to existing work.

The paper addresses an important question about the sources of generalization in neural networks and provides new theoretical tools (Theorem 2 and Theorem 3) that improve upon prior work. The key insight—that gradient descent selects large-margin solutions with vanishing posterior probability—is interesting and well-illustrated by the label-ray experiments. However, the theoretical derivations rely on strong assumptions (Gaussian priors, infinite-width limit, orthant geometry) that are not fully justified for practical deep networks, and the empirical validation is limited to small-scale MLPs on MNIST variants. The paper would benefit from clearer articulation of when the assumptions hold and from additional experiments that probe the boundaries of the theory.

Overview issues:

- Assumption that function space geometry is an orthant may not hold for deep nonlinear networks: Section 3.2 argues that in function space, the solution space V_S is simply an orthant (the set of sign patterns matching the training labels). This relies on the assumption that for any sign pattern, there exists a weight configuration realizing it. For infinitely wide networks with a Gaussian process prior, this is true almost surely under mild conditions. However, for finite-width networks trained by gradient descent, the function space F_S may be a proper subset of R^n, and the solution space may not be convex or even connected. The paper's theoretical results (Theorem 2, Theorem 3) are derived for the infinite-width NNGP case, but the experiments use finite-width networks. The paper acknowledges this gap but does not fully address whether the orthant geometry is a reasonable approximation for finite-width networks. A more careful discussion of when the orthant assumption breaks down and how that affects the conclusions would strengthen the paper.

- The Gaussian orthant probability bound (Lemma 1) relies on an unverified inequality: Lemma 1 provides a lower bound on the Gaussian orthant probability p (Equation 9) that involves the term (1/2 - 1/π) tr(Σ^{-1}) + (1/π) y^T Σ^{-1} y. The derivation in Appendix A.2 (not shown in the main text) must justify why this particular expression is a valid lower bound. The paper states that the bound follows from an inequality involving the exponential function, but the exact inequality is not stated in the main text. Without seeing the derivation, it is unclear whether the bound is tight or whether it might be vacuous for certain covariance matrices (e.g., near-singular Σ). The paper should explicitly state the inequality used and discuss its domain of validity. Additionally, the bound involves Σ^{-1}, which requires Σ to be invertible; for singular NNGP kernels (e.g., with repeated inputs), the bound may not apply, limiting its practical use.
